transcript: Pleasure to be here. And a little bit of a surprise, you know. This is actually my first Breakpoint and I'm coming to it as a speaker. I'm actually one of the people that Solana through Neon Labs and Neon EVM was able to convert from Ethereum space to to Solana. And I'll try to explain why in a couple of sentences. I was one of the—I was a part of Ethereum from basically early 2016. I was one of the people in charge of Polygon Edge, which used to be Polygon's EVM client, so I brought over 300 different blockchain use cases to Polygon Edge as a client, you know, back then. And after that, for the last two years up until recently when I became the CEO of Neon, I was in charge of strategy at Tenderly and brought, you know, Tenderly infrastructure and tooling to all of the EVM developers on over 100 EVM-based networks, mostly L2s. That's actually how I first heard about Neon EVM and started being interested, you know, in it a little bit more. And a couple of months later, what happened was I became the CEO of the company because of some interesting reasons. I think that it's extremely interesting and gratifying, you know, what happened with Neon EVM over time because when you look at it from the initial perspective, the main goal of Neon EVM when it started in, you know, late 2021 was that we are actually able to bring EVM developers, you know, that know how to write Solidity, to Solana as an ecosystem and to let them have their then existing dApps there. Didn't work out fully well. I would say the main reason why is that the main blocker for these people was actually not to learn Rust instead of Solidity. There are many constraints in Solana, you know, that make you change your EVM application business logic if you actually want it to run on Solana. And this is something where Neon EVM tried to do it through a then novel concept of Network Extensions. So we built an EVM inside of the SVM and said let's try, you know, to do this in the best possible way. However, the amount of those things that make Solana able to provide us with internet capital markets and to be Nasdaq on-chain makes it also very hard to make this fully possible. And when I look at it today, you know, this became also the reason why some people are now using, you know, Hyperliquid for example for high-frequency trading, but also why people that are rewriting their apps into Rust to run on Solana are also having problems changing their business logic to fit into this. And the reasoning is that Solana did a lot of amazing things to achieve this great speed. However, it also had to do a lot of different changes that made it less possible to run, you know, complex programs on Solana in full without offloading your computation to something like a network extension. So we decided as Neon Labs to fully enable now these reasons why people who have more complex DeFi apps are not maybe migrating to Solana yet, and we decided to help those that have existing Solana apps but want them to run at 10-20 milliseconds to maybe, you know, work with this. So we said that, you know, if we are talking about a network extension as a concept where you are still using your Solana wallet, you're still using Solana blockspace and Solana consensus, and you want to just seamlessly use off-chain computation to do this. And then we said, you know, well the only way for this system to work for us and remain trustless is actually to use Zero-Knowledge proofs. However, on Solana, Zero-Knowledge proofs are not that easy to do. With Ethereum being slow, you know, it's much easier for, you know, it to actually prove something using ZK and put stuff using ZK onto the main network. With Solana, it's much harder precisely because of the limitations of ZK technology itself, you know. It requires relatively large, slow, compute-heavy proofs. And when we look at it, you know, from this perspective, you know, even though we are seeing already some of the things that are going to get implemented to make Solana even faster even without off-chain computation, there are many things we can have today with off-chain computation such as application-controlled execution, which we will wait for a long time, you know, otherwise, that are possible actually today with ZK. But in Solana, it is a little bit tricky as I said. So we at Neon Labs, you know, increased our, you know, ZK capacity mostly through acquisitions. We tried to see what is working in Ethereum world and what is not because, you know, real ZK EVMs and ZK VMs are not also that common there. They have also a lot of problems of their own. And we sat down and tested out some of the examples of technologies we could actually use to bring you this. And each technology had a problem. However, we realized, you know, that, uh, you know, like a kid, you know, jumping through colored, uh, you know, squares, maybe we can do something to go through just the green part of this, uh, this picture, you know. And the thought about that was actually, you know, ZKs are just, uh, ZK circuits, you know, as a way to do this are basically just mathematical representations of computer programs. So if I run one ZK circuit customly built for that computer program and it generates a large proof—large proof, still less large than the program itself—I can then run another type of a ZK proving system on that proof and then prove that proof. So by running simultaneously one after another two different proving systems, we can actually, maybe in some cases even faster, prove something and make it small. So this is something we were able to verify we are completely able to do with Plonk, you know, and these are the results we achieved. So right now we can say that we are definitely capable of enabling real ZK in this manner on Solana through a network extension without you going to a Layer 2 or a different network. So you are just at the end of the day using your Solana wallet, you are not moving away, only the computation is. The second thing that we tested is that we are able to do this 10 times faster with GPUs. We are still working on our end goal of making this proving nearly instant. And we realized that by what I said, you know, we do need to write ZK circuits for each of these programs we are running. So our decision was at that point: what are the most used use cases right now? And if you are at Breakpoint today, you know that the most used use case is Perps because everyone is talking about Perps. There is lending also, there is, you know, spot—there are other things, but Perps are the way to go. So we said we cannot be just an engine, you know, we have to showcase this somehow. So we went on and are now, you know, finishing and building our specialized VM just for trading on Solana that will allow you to run a Perp CLOB DEX on Solana fully. It has to specialize the circuits, so for each of these we will have to run, you know, separate newly written ZK circuits, but, you know, in the endgame we hope that we will be able to build—and maybe some of you want to contribute—a general-purpose ZK solution for Solana like, you know, we have SNARKs and STARKs for example in Ethereum world. If you are interested, you know, you can check out Parasol, which is the engine that we were able to build. Please do, you know, scan the link, follow up, you know, register for the beta, and become people that might be interested in building on top of Parasol. We really invite you to first of all try building, you know, with us on top of Parasol, to try using our VM for trading. If you are building a DEX or if you are building any type of application that already has users but want to add a DEX to it, please do, you know, reach out to me and the rest of my team wearing these t-shirts and let's discuss it. Also if you are just interested in ZK and applications, you know, that you could run or contributing to a more open-source environment for something like this inside of Solana, do also reach out. We would love to help everyone who wants to migrate from either EVM or another ecosystem to Solana, and that was the main purpose of Neon Labs through Neon EVM, but now also through Parasol. Thank you all and really hope to speak to almost everyone of you.

enrichment.generated_query: Neon / Parasol Tech Talk: Neon Breakpoint 2025 announcement

tags.impact_reason: Neon EVM aims to bring EVM compatibility to Solana, which is a solid update for developers, even though initial goals weren't fully met and some apps still need rewriting.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a high-tech Parasol as the anchor because it's the product's name and serves as a powerful metaphor for a protective, functional layer (ZK-powered execution) built on top of the Solana base layer. The recursive, geometric patterns of its structure represent the ZK proofs. I included the text 'ZK' as a holographic emblem at the core of the structure because it's the key technology enabling this new capability, making it a diegetic and high-impact label for the engine itself.
visual_idea.description: A monumental, holographic parasol hovers in a dark, data-filled space. Its structural ribs are not solid, but composed of interlocking, recursive geometric patterns made of glowing neon-blue light, representing ZK proofs. The canopy is a shimmering, translucent membrane through which streams of teal and purple data flow, being processed and refined. In the background, far below, lies a representation of the Solana base layer—a sleek, minimalist server farm with a faint purple glow, stretching into the deep digital void. At the central apex of the parasol's complex structure, the letters 'ZK' glow brightly as a holographic, diegetic emblem, signifying the core technology.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, holographic parasol hovers majestically in a dark, data-filled digital space. Its structural ribs are not solid, but meticulously composed of interlocking, recursive geometric patterns, crafted from vibrant, glowing neon-blue light, reminiscent of an architectural blueprint, powerfully representing ZK proofs. The canopy of this parasol is a shimmering, bioluminescent, translucent membrane through which intricate streams of teal and purple data flow, actively being processed and refined. In the background, far below, lies an expansive representation of the Solana base layer—a sleek, minimalist server farm with a faint purple glow, stretching endlessly into the deep digital void, emphasizing epic scale and height, with ample negative space above the parasol. At the central apex of the parasol's complex structure, the letters 'ZK' glow brightly as a volumetric holographic, diegetic emblem, signifying the core technology. The overall lighting emanates from the internal glowing elements and data streams. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), global illumination, ambient occlusion, monumental scale, vertical composition.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a high-tech Parasol as the anchor because it's the product's name and serves as a powerful metaphor for a protective, functional layer (ZK-powered execution) built on top of the Solana base layer. The recursive, geometric patterns of its structure represent the ZK proofs. I included the text 'ZK' as a holographic emblem at the core of the structure because it's the key technology enabling this new capability, making it a diegetic and high-impact label for the engine itself.
visual_idea.description: A super-macro shot focusing on the intricate joint of the Parasol's architecture. A glowing, neon-blue holographic rib, etched with fractal-like geometric patterns, connects to the semi-transparent, shimmering canopy. Streams of light, representing high-frequency transaction data, are visible coursing through the canopy's membrane, refracting and bending as they pass through the complex structure.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A super-macro shot focusing on the intricate joint of the Parasol's architecture. A glowing, vibrant neon-blue holographic rib, precisely etched with complex fractal-like geometric patterns and accentuated with cyan glow outlines, seamlessly connects to the semi-transparent, shimmering bioluminescent canopy. Within the membrane of the canopy, vivid streams of light, representing high-frequency transaction data, are clearly visible, coursing and flowing, refracting and bending dynamically as they pass through the intricate, complex structure. The composition is symmetrical and centrally focused, tightly framed to fill the entire square (1:1) aspect ratio with rich texture and microscopic details, showcasing a blend of architectural blueprint precision and futuristic technology. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering, extreme ultra detail.

editorial.headline: Neon Unveils Parasol: ZK-Powered Execution for High-Frequency Solana DeFi
editorial.standfirst: Neon pivots from pure EVM compatibility to "Network Extensions" with the launch of Parasol, a ZK engine enabling complex compute for on-chain derivatives. The architecture uses recursive proofs to bypass Solana’s verification limits, keeping liquidity unified on the base layer.
key_takeaways: **Product Launch**: Neon introduces **Parasol**, a ZK-compute engine acting as a "Network Extension" to offload execution while settling on Solana.
**Technical Breakthrough**: Solves Solana's ZK verification constraints by using recursive proofs—verifying a heavy proof with a lighter **Plonk** proof.
**First Application**: A specialized Virtual Machine designed specifically for **CLOB perpetuals**, aiming to rival off-chain performance.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
Neon Labs has fundamentally expanded its mandate beyond bringing Ethereum developers to Solana. In a technical keynote at Breakpoint 2025, CEO Ivan Bjelajac admitted that the "language barrier" was never the primary obstacle for migration; rather, it was the execution constraints for complex business logic on the Solana Virtual Machine (SVM). To solve this, Neon is pivoting to a "Network Extension" model with the launch of **Parasol**, a Zero-Knowledge (ZK) compute engine designed to handle heavy execution off-chain while retaining Solana as the settlement layer.

The core innovation of Parasol lies in its approach to Solana's compute budget limitations. While Solana is fast, verifying large, compute-heavy ZK proofs on-chain is historically difficult. Parasol utilizes a recursive proving architecture: it generates a custom circuit proof for the program execution and then validates that proof using a lighter, faster system like **Plonk**. This "proof of a proof" compresses the data sufficiently to fit within a standard Solana transaction, enabling trustless off-chain computation without the latency or fragmentation associated with Layer 2 rollups.

This architecture targets the most demanding sector of the current market: on-chain derivatives. Bjelajac revealed that Parasol's first deployment will be a specialized Virtual Machine optimized for Central Limit Order Book (CLOB) perpetuals. By offloading the matching engine to Parasol's ZK environment—accelerated by GPUs for 10x performance—developers can build high-frequency trading platforms that rival centralized exchanges or app-chains like Hyperliquid, all while keeping user funds and liquidity unified on the Solana mainnet.
